facts.text: OpenAPI Specification (OAS) is a standard, programming language-agnostic interface description for HTTP APIs that enables both humans and computers to discover and understand service capabilities without accessing source code
Contract-first development allows teams to define the API interface before implementing business logic, enabling contract-driven testing and automated integration tests

HTTP 422 (Unprocessable Entity) is semantically distinct from 400—400 indicates structural/syntactic errors (malformed JSON), while 422 indicates syntactically valid payloads that fail domain validation rules
The first digit of HTTP status codes defines response class: 1xx (informational), 2xx (success), 3xx (redirection), 4xx (client error), 5xx (server error)
Offset-based pagination costs scale linearly with offset value—OFFSET 99980 requires scanning and discarding 99,980 rows even when requesting only 20 rows
Cursor-based pagination uses opaque position markers (often derived from last item's ID) instead of offsets, remaining stable under concurrent inserts/deletes—new items appear only on future pages
Stripe's list API uses cursor-based pagination with limit (1-100), starting_after (cursor forward), and ending_before (cursor backward) parameters; responses include has_more boolean
Contentful's cursor pagination implementation encodes all query parameters except limit into the opaque cursor token; limit can be changed between pages but other filters are locked
Recommended REST API nesting depth is maximum 3 levels; deeper nesting creates brittle URLs that are difficult to evolve and maintain
API versioning strategies include URI path versioning (/v1/, /v2/), header-based versioning (Accept-Version header), and query parameter versioning (?version=2)
Typical API deprecation timeline: 6-month announcement period, 12 months active migration support, 18-24 months total before version removal
OpenAPI 3.1 introduced the deprecate keyword to communicate deprecations as an API evolves, supporting API evolution without full version breaks

HTTP 409 Conflict indicates the request conflicts with the current resource state (concurrent updates, duplicate creation). Optimistic concurrency control uses ETags and If-Match headers to prevent blind overwrites
Stripe's OpenAPI specification is publicly available on GitHub and enables automatic generation of SDKs in multiple programming languages (Rust, D, etc.)
OpenAPI Generator supports 50+ language targets for client code generation, using operation IDs and tags to organize generated code—operation IDs must be unique across all endpoints
Cursor pagination can achieve 17x performance improvement over offset pagination on large datasets—query performance stays consistent regardless of dataset size
Netflix transitioned from monolithic API → direct microservice access → gateway aggregation → federated GraphQL architecture to handle scaling across thousands of data consumers
RFC 9110 (IETF June 2022) elevated HTTP 422 from WebDAV extension (RFC 4918) to Internet Standard, renaming it from 'Unprocessable Entity' to 'Unprocessable Content'
Consumer-Driven Contract (CDC) testing isolates provider-consumer interactions using mocks that conform to a shared contract specification (JSON format from Pact)
GraphQL enables clients to request exactly the data they need in a single query, eliminating over-fetching and multiple round-trips required by REST's fixed response shapes

examples.context: GitHub API returns 422 when creating a team with a non-unique name: 'POST api.github.com/orgs/my-org/teams: 422 Validation Failed [{Resource:Team Field:data Code:unprocessable Message:Name must be unique for this org}]'
examples.lesson: Status codes must distinguish structural errors (400) from semantic validation failures (422)

examples.context: GitHub API returns 422 when uploaded SSH key already exists on account with 'The key is already in use' message
examples.lesson: Resource uniqueness constraints should return 422, not 400

examples.context: PostgreSQL OFFSET pagination: querying a 50M row table with OFFSET 99980 takes 8 seconds. Cursor pagination on same table returns in 0.01 seconds
examples.lesson: Offset pagination performance degrades exponentially with offset value; cursor pagination is stable

examples.context: Stripe API lists objects with limit (1-100), returns has_more boolean, uses starting_after and ending_before for pagination
examples.lesson: Cursor-based design prevents duplicate/skipped records under concurrent writes

examples.context: Contentful migrated entire content APIs to cursor pagination; query parameters (except limit) are encoded into cursor and locked—cannot change filters between pages
examples.lesson: Cursors must encode all filtering state to remain stable across requests

examples.context: Netflix API evolved through 4 stages: Monolith → Direct Microservice Access → Gateway Aggregation → Federated GraphQL with Domain Graph Services (DGS)
examples.lesson: Hierarchical resource design scales by decentralizing schema definition to domain teams

examples.context: REST API endpoints using GET /users/{id}/delete violate HTTP semantics. Correct pattern: DELETE /users/{id}
examples.lesson: HTTP methods must express intent, not hide operations in paths

examples.context: Common mistake: returning 200 OK for all responses including errors. Correct: use 4xx for client errors, 5xx for server errors
examples.lesson: Status codes are part of the contract; clients depend on them for logic

analogies.analogy: REST resource nesting is like file system paths: /users/{id}/posts/{postId} creates clear hierarchy. But excessive nesting (/users/{id}/posts/{postId}/comments/{commentId}/reactions/{reactionId}) becomes brittle, like deeply nested folders that break when renamed.

Offset pagination is like reading a book starting from page 100: you must read sequentially from the beginning (page 1) to reach page 100, even though you only want that one page. Cursor pagination is like bookmarks: jump directly to where you left off.
HTTP status codes are like exit codes in Unix: 2xx = success (exit 0), 4xx = client error (exit 1-9 range), 5xx = program error (exit 10+). Clients parse these signals to decide what to do next.

API versioning is like semantic versioning in code: major.minor.patch. Major versions break clients (v1 → v2), minor versions add features (1.1 → 1.2), patches fix bugs (1.2.1 → 1.2.2). Deprecation is the migration period between versions.
OpenAPI specs are like type signatures in typed languages: they describe inputs, outputs, and errors. Code generators read these signatures and produce client libraries, eliminating boilerplate.

Cursor pagination under concurrent writes is like maintaining a position in a deck of cards: even if cards are inserted before your position, you still know which card comes next. Offset pagination is like counting from the start each time—inserting a card shuffles positions.

key_numbers.value: 17x faster than offset pagination on large datasets

key_numbers.context: Each offset value scans and discards that many rows

key_numbers.metric: Cursor pagination on same 50M row table

key_numbers.context: Deeper nesting creates brittleness and maintenance burden
Including Go, Rust, Python, TypeScript, Java, C#, PHP, etc.

key_numbers.metric: HTTP status code first digit meaning
key_numbers.context: 1xx: info, 2xx: success, 3xx: redirect, 4xx: client error, 5xx: server error

key_numbers.value: 6 months announcement + 12 months migration support + 6 months sunset = 24 months total
key_numbers.context: Industry best practice for breaking changes
Drove evolution from monolith to federated GraphQL

common_misconceptions.misconception: Offset pagination works fine for any dataset size
common_misconceptions.truth: Offset pagination performance degrades linearly with offset value. At OFFSET 1,000,000, the database scans 1M rows to discard them. For large datasets, cursor pagination is mandatory.

common_misconceptions.misconception: HTTP 400 is the right status code for all validation errors
common_misconceptions.truth: 400 is for malformed requests (structural errors). 422 is for valid syntax with semantic/business rule violations. GitHub, Stripe, and others distinguish them.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Deeper REST nesting improves code organization (e.g., /orgs/{id}/teams/{id}/members/{id}/roles/{id})
common_misconceptions.truth: Deep nesting creates brittle URLs that break during refactors. Recommended max is 3 levels; beyond that use separate endpoints with references.

common_misconceptions.misconception: 200 OK response means no error occurred
common_misconceptions.truth: 200 is only semantically correct for successful requests. Returning 200 with error details in the body breaks HTTP contract. Use 4xx/5xx for errors.

common_misconceptions.misconception: OpenAPI specs are only for documentation
common_misconceptions.truth: OpenAPI is a contract-first specification. It enables mock servers, automated tests, SDK generation, and client-server testing without coupling.

common_misconceptions.misconception: REST APIs should use POST for everything to be flexible
common_misconceptions.truth: HTTP methods encode semantics: GET (safe, cacheable), POST (creates resource), PUT (replaces), PATCH (modifies), DELETE (removes). Using POST for everything breaks HTTP caching and client expectations.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Cursor pagination requires exposing record IDs to clients
common_misconceptions.truth: Cursors are opaque tokens (often base64-encoded JSON) that encode position and query state. Clients never inspect their contents.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Versioning an API means creating completely separate codebases (v1, v2, v3)
common_misconceptions.truth: API evolution maintains backward compatibility via additive changes. Full version breaks are last resort. OpenAPI 3.1's deprecate keyword enables gradual transitions.

common_misconceptions.misconception: GraphQL solves all API design problems
common_misconceptions.truth: GraphQL eliminates over-fetching but complicates caching (POST-based, no URL-based HTTP caching), security (field-level access control complexity), and introduces query complexity attacks.

common_misconceptions.misconception: OpenAPI is only useful for REST APIs
common_misconceptions.truth: OpenAPI 3.1 extends to webhooks, callbacks, and multi-step workflows (Arazzo). The spec is evolving beyond pure REST.
